_: Volume -2 presents ABC State and Educere practices that help young people learn self-discipline through social responsibility. Instead of following punitive consequence procedures against individuals, these practices help students and teachers develop soft skills to mutually generate a healthy social environment — setting the foundational experience for decentralising regulation of social behaviour, which may permeate and influence hierarchies in traditional adult organisations. The structure empowers students to collectively determine a positive learning relationship, transforming teachers from disciplinarians into learning facilitators.

1._: The verso shares a decade of experiential discovery through teacher locumming — approaching the discipline problem from a deliberately different angle by refusing full-time positions, instead moving between schools to learn how engagement works without imposing will on students. The method: ABC Classes, where the collective behaviour of students determines the teacher's role.

Opening gambit 'Rhubarb': the author begins a first lesson with bottom-set GCSE students speaking nonsense until the entire class has turned away. 'That's what it's like, isn't it? Maths is like a different language. And if you don't speak it, it just sounds like nonsense.' Relief — someone finally speaking to the truth of their experience. Then 'On Yer Bike' algebra (V-1) transforms their relationship to the subject.

'We're not afraid of you': when asked why they don't behave as well for the author as for the fearsome deputy head, a student explains directly. The insight: the best teachers avoid conflict by engendering respect, but this makes the how of discipline invisible — you never see examples of what to do when things go wrong. The author decided to learn engagement with minimal enforcement and maximal learning.
1.1: Attention as gift, not demand: standing at the doorway greeting students, engaging the confident, gently waking the avoidant, letting the rest settle — then turning to find them all looking, silent and calm. 'I didn't have to ask for your attention. I certainly didn't need to demand it. You gave me your attention.' Such a rare thing — like looking at the sea, sparkling with sunlight, the air completely still.

2._: ABC Classes — like water having different states, so does psycho-social reality. A-state: everyone has self-discipline, capable of varied activities, quick turnarounds, adventure. B-state: someone loses self-discipline but when pointed out they correct it — peers helping peers. C-state: someone loses self-discipline, it's pointed out, they continue — teacher enforces sanctions. The collective behaviour determines the teacher's role.

1._: Critical distinction: C*-state (well-behaved because of teacher's implicit threat) is not A-state. Test: replace the teacher — an A-state class retains form, a C*-state degrades without the imposing presence. The teacher cannot create an A-state class; they can only create conditions for it to emerge. Determining the state is part of the intervention — the teacher has final say because it determines their subsequent preparation.

The system works on a feedback loop: if a class achieves B or A-state, the teacher prepares more challenging and varied activities for next lesson. If C-state, single big task with imposed control. Students learn that their collective behaviour opens or closes possibilities. I give the benefit of the doubt and treat every class as B-state.
1.1: 'The Best Class In The World': a visitor arrives, the author walks away saying 'introduce yourselves, class.' A student pipes up 'we're the best class in the school', then 'Scotland', then 'the universe'. Then a formerly disruptive boy, now sitting centre-class, says to the visitor: 'We, my class, did twenty-five weeks work in five.' And as he says it, he looks down — dissolving attention from himself into the class. You cannot teach someone to do this. Authentic emergence from natural social ability finding its natural place.

2.2: Classroom Coordinators: group-dynamic specialists complementing subject teachers, diagnosing social weaknesses, teaching metacognitive tools, mediation and negotiation. After establishing A-state by Christmas, they step away to support older, more challenging classes. A highly preventative strategy — good practice instilled early avoids costly problem-solving later. Our responsibility as educators: no longer just preparing workers, but participants in a healthy sustainable world.

3._: Self-discipline has four aspects: I control myself (therefore no-one exerts control over me), I am responsible for my behaviour (distance between self and behaviour), I am accountable (if forced to do something wrong, challenge it appropriately), and self-discipline can be encouraged through group work, reflective exercises, performances, and continuous ABC feedback.
3.1: Social inclusion as social responsibility: students perform for their peers, not teachers. If peers are held accountable for the individual's behaviour, they won't encourage disruption — the group acts as buffer zone. A student supported in maths may support others in English or football. The mutually supportive network helps students understand the benefit of groupings at every scale, eventually understanding why they've been at school.

4._: Power dynamics as exhaustive relationality: power-over as vertical differential (I>you), power-with as horizontal (I=you or voluntary I<you). Two people A & B generate three dynamic pairings: competition (both I>you — conflict), fellowship (both I<you — distance/invitation), and dominant-submissive (A>B matched with B<A — institutionalised hierarchy). The A>B pairing defines C-state and when chained becomes centralised hierarchy of near-all adult organisations.

1._: The key to B-state is in voluntary I<you submission: the student makes the teacher, the follower makes the leader. Children follow one another naturally — whoever has the best story, funniest perspective, most skill. Leadership is fluid, dependent on context. Flocking, murmuration. For stable B-state, be vigilant that I>you is not institutionalised; one bad apple spoils the barrel.

A-state occurs when sufficient I=you relations exist — not institutional descriptions of equality (which become part of power-over bureaucracy) but the lived forgiveness of individuals who have self-discipline for those who momentarily lose it. A-state is the social equivalent of flow-state, induced by concurrent relational behaviour, not described or enforced.
1.1: The task of describing A-state directly is defeated by description itself. Non-verbal environments reveal the difference: martial arts (receiving an incoming fist as invitation to dance, not executing a trained block), tango (natural joint articulation, not rehearsed moves), music (improvisation vs performing from composition), Go (discovering a divine move together vs chess opponents facing off). The temporal quality distinguishes power-with from power-over.

1.5: Origins: postmodern journeyman — accepted to study pure maths at Cambridge, instead completed Social Anthropology at Edinburgh, took teaching as engaged ethnography rather than career. Locumming to approach the problem from a different angle: 'you shouldn't give cake to the kids — they end up throwing it around.' The fun activities only work when collectively earned. Standard consequence system = divide and conquer. ABC State = distributed self-correction.

2._: The recto situates ABC State academically as filling the gap between child-centred learning theory and the practicalities of running a class with thirty 'centres of learning' — providing the systemic social equivalent to the punitive consequence procedure that education currently lacks, grounded in dissipated control theory (Doll via Kauffman), self-regulation research, and operational complexity.

1._: The problem: discipline remains a central issue — students lose up to an hour of learning daily from disruption (OFSTED 2014). Four strategies exist: engaging lessons, punitive consequences, teacher soft-skills, and direct social learning — but tensions between them compound the problem. Discipline demotivates (Payne 2015, Baird 2010), soft-skills may create 'illusion of freedom' constituting structural violence (Matusov & Sullivan 2019, Graeber 2015), and direct SEL teaching is ineffective with adolescents (Yeager 2017).

There is no systemic equivalent to the consequence procedure of escalating sanctions — no common language of relationality (beyond verbally complex codes of conduct), no simple mechanism for self-organisation or social cohesion. Low-level disruption is evaluated as failure of discipline policy rather than failure of relationships.
1.1: Etymology of 'discipline' reveals the deep marriage of expectation and consequence: from Latin disciplina (instruction, knowledge) and discipulus (pupil) and discere (to learn), through Old French descepline (12th-13th century: punishment for correction), to 14th century programme of regulations, 15th century military training, 16th century church conduct. Discipline as knowledge and discipline as punishment became fused — the coupling that ABC State aims to uncouple.

2._: Doll's 'Complexity in the Classroom' (2000, 2012) applies Kauffman's self-organisation heuristic: systems emerge when there is (a) critical active mass, (b) only a few operating connections at any time, (c) a simple set of operating rules. Mapped to classrooms: (a) viable active group of learner-students including teacher, (b) emergence of varying interconnected dynamic foci, (c) few basic procedures for iterative connection of interactive energies.

1._: This 'dissipated control' correlates to A-state. Doll: 'Control is dissipated into the group, community, network, system, and indeed frames itself. Lesson plans are nonlinear and recursive. Details dealing with organisation, conduct, evaluation are all the result of reflections on occurrences rather than pre-set formulae.' Authority moves from external institutional source into the situation itself (Dewey 1966/1916).
1.1: ABC State provides the 'measure' of self-organisation that Doll falls short of — the closest he gets is 'reflective habits' and 'a time-developmental process of cooperative interaction'. ABC Classes makes self-organisation observable, gradable, and actionable for both teachers and students within standard school environments.

3._: Educere as systemic learning: 1:5 fission-fusion (friendship groups of five as basic social unit), meta-learning (learning how to learn across subjects), investigative learning and cross-curricular integration, and the seven-year cycle (building social capacity from primary through secondary into self-organising young adults).

1._: Modular pay and retirement: when operating a value-tracking economy (V3) where income distributes to everyone who has been helpful, teaching becomes one of the most financially secure professions — the act of teaching is one's retirement plan, the better you do the more rewarded in old age. Leading Teachers refers to the profession's unique position: greatest resource (young minds), daily experience of complex communication systems, producing the next generation.
1.1: Institutional transparency through qualitative evaluations reflecting two of three critical factors: are students looking forward to the lesson? Are teachers looking forward to the class? Are students achieving potential? Future-proofing: with AI replacing millions of jobs, the more human soft-skills of intention, compassion, communication become prime.

4._: Four vectors of implementation: parent push (traditionally little influence on daily school activity), solitary teacher adoption (complements standard systems), whole-school policy (suffers from replication problem — absurd to enforce change), and Classroom Coordinators (organic learning in situ, A-state network growing person-to-person). Most powerful driver: student demand.
4.1: The question for the 2030s: can we prepare a generation who have learned to interact within teams, compete against other teams, settle into self-organised practices with strangers, and gather in larger groups? Equipped with collaborative business practices (V2) and value-tracking (V3), might they self-organise a polity in the millions? Is this feasible through classroom teachers, specialist Coordinators, or only through impartial AI? A decision every educator must make.
